section.title: From Adam to Noah
section.sectionRationale: The book of the generations of Adam carries the promised line from Adam to Noah in ten steps. The steady refrain, and then he died, tolls like a bell through the chapter, broken only by Enoch, who walked with God and was taken. It is a single, self-contained genealogy opening with humanity made in God's likeness and closing on Noah, who brings hope.
section.thesis: Death reigns through the generations from Adam, yet the promised line holds, one man walks with God and escapes death, and the chapter ends looking to Noah for comfort.
section.frameNote: Four movements: humanity made in God's likeness; the tolling line from Adam to Jared; Enoch who walked with God and was taken; and the descent to Noah, named for comfort.

verses.text: This is the book of the generations of Adam. In the day that God created man, He made him in His own {{likeness|likeness}}.
Male and female He created them, and He blessed them. And in the day they were created, He called them “man.”

units.label: The tolling line from Adam to Jared

verses.text: When Adam was 130 years old, he had a son in his own likeness, after his own image; and he named him Seth.
And after he had become the father of Seth, Adam lived 800 years and had other sons and daughters.
So Adam lived a total of 930 years, and then he {{died|died}}.
When Seth was 105 years old, he became the father of Enosh.
And after he had become the father of Enosh, Seth lived 807 years and had other sons and daughters.
So Seth lived a total of 912 years, and then he died.
When Enosh was 90 years old, he became the father of Kenan.
And after he had become the father of Kenan, Enosh lived 815 years and had other sons and daughters.
So Enosh lived a total of 905 years, and then he died.
When Kenan was 70 years old, he became the father of Mahalalel.
And after he had become the father of Mahalalel, Kenan lived 840 years and had other sons and daughters.
So Kenan lived a total of 910 years, and then he died.
When Mahalalel was 65 years old, he became the father of Jared.
And after he had become the father of Jared, Mahalalel lived 830 years and had other sons and daughters.
So Mahalalel lived a total of 895 years, and then he died.
When Jared was 162 years old, he became the father of Enoch.
And after he had become the father of Enoch, Jared lived 800 years and had other sons and daughters.
So Jared lived a total of 962 years, and then he died.
When Enoch was 65 years old, he became the father of Methuselah.
And after he had become the father of Methuselah, Enoch {{walked|walked with God}} 300 years and had other sons and daughters.
So Enoch lived a total of 365 years.
Enoch walked with God, and then he was no more, because God had {{took|taken him away}}.
When Methuselah was 187 years old, he became the father of Lamech.
And after he had become the father of Lamech, Methuselah lived 782 years and had other sons and daughters.
So Methuselah lived a total of 969 years, and then he died.
When Lamech was 182 years old, he had a son.
And he named him Noah, saying, “May this one {{comfort|comfort}} us in the labor and toil of our hands caused by the ground that the LORD has cursed.”
And after he had become the father of Noah, Lamech lived 595 years and had other sons and daughters.
So Lamech lived a total of 777 years, and then he died.
After Noah was 500 years old, he became the father of Shem, Ham, and Japheth.

likeness.gloss: Likeness, resemblance. Humanity is made in God's likeness, and Adam then fathers a son in his own likeness. The image of God is dented by the fall yet still passed down the generations, so every person in this list carries a God-given dignity even under the reign of death.

died.gloss: To die. The refrain and then he died closes almost every entry, the drumbeat fulfillment of God's warning in the day you eat of it you will surely die. Long lives cannot outrun it; death reigns over the line of Adam and waits for a rescuer.

walked.gloss: To walk, to go about one's life. To walk with God describes an intimate, faithful life lived in step with Him, the same phrase later used of Noah. Enoch's walk breaks the chapter's refrain of death and shows that fellowship with God is possible even in a dying world.

took.gloss: To take, to receive, to fetch. God took Enoch, and he did not see death. The same verb describes how the LORD would later take up Elijah, and it hints at a hope beyond the grave for those who walk with God, drawn out in Hebrews 11:5.

comfort.gloss: To comfort, to bring relief. Lamech names his son Noah, hoping he will comfort them from the toil of the cursed ground. The longing for relief from the curse runs through the chapter and reaches far beyond Noah to the true comfort found in Christ.

basic.context: This chapter is a family tree from Adam down to Noah. It begins by reminding us that people are made in God's likeness. Then it lists ten generations, and after almost every name it says the same thing, and then he died. The question is whether anything can break that sad pattern.
basic.hermeneutics: It really is a genealogy, but it is arranged to preach. The repeated and then he died shows that God's warning about death came true. The key question is where hope will come from, and the chapter answers with two bright spots, Enoch who walked with God, and Noah, named for comfort.
basic.originalLanguages: People still bear God's likeness, demut, even after the fall. But the word died, muth, tolls again and again, fulfilling God's warning. One man, Enoch, walked, halak, with God, and God took, laqach, him so he did not die. And Noah is named for comfort, nacham, from the cursed ground.
basic.typology: Enoch, taken up without dying, is a small picture of resurrection hope and of Christ's own ascension and the future taking up of God's people. Noah, whose name means comfort, foreshadows the greater rest and comfort that only Christ can bring from the curse.
basic.christ: Enoch's walk with God and rescue from death point ahead to the life and victory found in Jesus. And the longing for comfort from the curse, spoken over Noah, is finally answered in Christ, who bore the curse and gives rest to the weary.
basic.god: God gives life and dignity, calling humanity by name and blessing them. He keeps His word about death, yet He also delights to walk with people and to take Enoch to Himself. God is faithful to carry the promised line safely down to Noah.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 11 says Enoch was taken up by faith and pleased God. Luke 3 traces Jesus' family back through this very list. Jude quotes Enoch as a prophet. Romans 5 explains why death reigned from Adam.
basic.oneStory: Death reigns from Adam through these ten generations, exactly as God had warned. But the promised line does not fail, one man walks with God and escapes the grave, and the chapter ends looking to Noah for comfort, a hope that presses on toward Christ, who defeats death itself.

context.literary: The first full toledot, the book of the generations of Adam, structured as ten symmetrical entries with a repeated formula, punctuated by the refrain and then he died and broken uniquely by Enoch. It deliberately echoes the language of creation and the fall.
context.historical: For Israel, this list anchored their identity in a real line from Adam and explained the great ages of the ancients while insisting that even they died. It links the promise of a seed to a traceable family reaching Noah and the flood.
context.canonical: This genealogy carries the promised seed of 3:15 forward and is picked up directly in the genealogy of Jesus in Luke 3, tying the whole line from Adam through Noah to Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To trace the promised line from Adam to Noah, to show that death now reigns over humanity as God warned, and to hold out hope through Enoch's walk with God and Noah's name of comfort.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter is largely descriptive record, yet Enoch stands as a quiet model, a life that walked with God is what pleases Him and what the reader is drawn to imitate.

debates.question: How should we understand the very long lifespans in this chapter?

views.label: Literal ages in a different early world
views.case: The numbers are given plainly as real ages, and the pre-flood world may have been physically different; the text presents them as straightforward history with no hint of exaggeration.

views.label: Meaningful or schematic numbers
views.case: Some of the figures carry symbolic weight, such as Enoch's 365 years matching the solar year and Lamech's 777, suggesting the numbers may also communicate theological meaning alongside record.

debates.bearing: On either view the chapter's central claim stands unshaken, these are real people in a real line, and despite their long lives, death still comes for all but the man who was taken.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is framed by two great words and lit by two others. It opens on demut, likeness: humanity bears God's likeness, and Adam passes that likeness on, so the dignity of the image survives the fall. But over that dignity falls the shadow of muth, to die, the refrain that closes nearly every life, the ringing proof that God's warning came true. Then the drumbeat stops for Enoch, who halak, walked, with God until God laqach, took, him, and he did not see death. The chapter ends on nacham, comfort, as Lamech names Noah in longing for relief from the cursed ground, a longing the whole Bible will carry to Christ.

typology.fulfillment: A foreshadow of resurrection and ascension, fulfilled in Christ who conquers death and in the future catching up of His people, Hebrews 11:5, 1 Thessalonians 4:17.
Christ, who truly comforts and gives rest from the curse and toil, bearing the curse Himself, Matthew 11:28, Galatians 3:13.

typology.type: The likeness of God passed down the line
typology.fulfillment: Restored and perfected in Christ, the true image of God, into whose likeness His people are remade, Colossians 3:10.

study.christ: Two figures in this chapter reach toward Jesus. Enoch, who walked with God and was taken so that he did not see death, is a signpost to the life beyond the grave that Christ secures; Hebrews 11:5 makes him a hero of faith who pleased God, and his rescue anticipates both Christ's own ascension and the promise that the dead in Christ will be raised and caught up. Noah, named for comfort from the cursed ground, points to the greater Comforter who bears the curse Himself and calls the weary to find rest in Him. Even the tolling of death through this list sets the stage for the last Adam, in whom death is finally undone.
study.god: God is the giver of life and dignity, creating humanity male and female, blessing them, and naming them. He is faithful to His warning, for death now reigns as He said it would; He is no less faithful to His promise, carrying the chosen line unbroken from Adam to Noah. And He is the God who delights in fellowship, walking with Enoch and taking him to Himself. Judgment and mercy, warning and promise, all rest in the same steady hand that guides the generations.

crossReferences.note: By faith Enoch was taken up so that he did not see death, for he was commended as one who pleased God.
The genealogy of Jesus runs directly through this line from Noah back to Adam.
Death reigned from Adam onward, exactly the pattern this chapter records.
Enoch, the seventh from Adam, prophesied of the Lord's coming in judgment.

study.oneStory: From Adam the line runs on through ten generations, and over each one falls the same shadow, and then he died, the reign of death that entered at the fall. Yet the promised seed is not lost: it passes safely down to Noah. One man walks with God and escapes the grave, and the chapter closes in longing for comfort from the curse. That longing, and that unbroken line, press on toward Jesus, the last Adam, who breaks the refrain of death for good.

quiz.q: What refrain repeats through most of the chapter, and what does it teach?

options.text: And then he died, teaching that death now reigns over Adam's line as God warned.
options.note: The refrain drums home the fulfillment of God's warning about death.

options.text: And he walked with God, teaching that everyone pleased the LORD.
options.note: That phrase is used only of Enoch, the exception, not the rule of the chapter.

options.text: And he was fruitful, teaching that the population grew.
options.note: Growth is present, but the defining refrain is death, not fruitfulness.

options.text: And the LORD blessed him, teaching only prosperity.
options.note: The recurring note is death, not blessing.

quiz.q: What is unique about Enoch in this genealogy?

options.text: He walked with God and did not die; God took him away.
options.note: Enoch alone breaks the refrain of death.

options.text: He lived the longest of all the fathers listed.
options.note: The opposite; his 365 years are the shortest span, and he did not die at all.

options.text: He was the only one who had sons and daughters.
options.note: Many in the list had sons and daughters; Enoch's uniqueness is walking with God and being taken.

options.text: He refused to have any children.
options.note: The text says Enoch fathered Methuselah and other sons and daughters.

quiz.q: Why did Lamech name his son Noah?

options.text: He hoped this son would bring comfort from the toil of the cursed ground.
options.note: Noah's name is tied to comfort and relief from the curse.

options.text: Because Noah means mighty warrior.
options.note: Noah is linked to rest or comfort, not to warfare.

options.text: To honor his grandfather Enoch.
options.note: The naming is about longed-for comfort, not honoring an ancestor.

options.text: For no stated reason at all.
options.note: The text gives a clear reason, comfort from the cursed ground.

quiz.q: How does Enoch point forward to the gospel?

options.text: Being taken up without dying, he anticipates resurrection hope and Christ's victory over death.
options.note: Hebrews 11:5 makes Enoch a sign of life beyond the grave through faith.

options.text: He wrote the first book of the Bible.
options.note: The text does not say this; his link to the gospel is his rescue from death.

options.text: He built an altar and offered sacrifice.
options.note: That detail is not in the chapter; his significance is walking with God and being taken.

options.text: He has no connection to the gospel.
options.note: Hebrews holds him up as a man of faith who pleased God.

quiz.q: What truth does the chapter affirm about human beings at its very start?

options.text: They are made in God's likeness, and that dignity is passed down the generations.
options.note: Verses 1 to 3 anchor human dignity in the image of God.

options.text: They are mere accidents with no special worth.
options.note: The chapter says the opposite; humanity bears God's likeness.

options.text: They were created only to work the ground.
options.note: Work is real, but the chapter's opening truth is being made in God's likeness.

options.text: They lost the image of God completely at the fall.
options.note: The likeness is dented yet still passed on, as Adam fathers a son in his likeness.

questions: The words and then he died land again and again in this chapter. How does honestly facing your own mortality change the way you want to live this year?
Enoch simply walked with God, day after day, for three hundred years. What would it look like for your ordinary days to become a steady walk with God rather than an occasional visit?
Lamech longed for someone to bring comfort from a hard, cursed life of toil. Where are you weary right now, and how might you bring that longing to Christ, who gives rest?
This chapter insists that every person, even under the reign of death, still bears God's likeness. How should that shape the way you treat the people you will meet this week?
The promised line held together through generations of loss. Where do you need to trust that God is quietly keeping His promises across the long stretch of your family's story?

translationNotes.note: He was no more, because God had taken him away renders a Hebrew phrase the Greek Old Testament reads as he was not found, because God had taken him; Hebrews 11:5 follows that reading to say Enoch did not see death.
The name Noah sounds like the Hebrew word for rest or comfort, and Lamech's saying, may this one comfort us, plays on that sound; the wordplay ties Noah to relief from the curse of 3:17.
